scenario: Japanese A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
text: In Japanese, how do you say 'To be - Polite'? {{c1::です (desu)}}
explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Personal Pronouns and To Be'. Level: A1.
usage: Term: です (desu) | Meaning: To be - Polite
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'To be - Polite' en Japanese? です (desu)

scenario: Japanese A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
text: In Japanese, how do you say 'To not be - Polite'? {{c1::ではありません (dewa arimasen)}}
explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Personal Pronouns and To Be'. Level: A1.
usage: Term: ではありません (dewa arimasen) | Meaning: To not be - Polite
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'To not be - Polite' en Japanese? ではありません (dewa arimasen)

scenario: Japanese A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
text: In Japanese, how do you say 'Is it?'? {{c1::ですか (desu ka?)}}
explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Personal Pronouns and To Be'. Level: A1.
usage: Term: ですか (desu ka?) | Meaning: Is it?
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'Is it?' en Japanese? ですか (desu ka?)

scenario: Japanese A1: Personal Pronouns and To Be 🗣️
text: In Japanese, how do you say 'I am a student'? {{c1::学生です (gakusei desu)}}
explanation: Vocabulary for the topic 'Personal Pronouns and To Be'. Level: A1.
usage: Term: 学生です (gakusei desu) | Meaning: I am a student
spanish: ¿Cómo se dice 'I am a student' en Japanese? 学生です (gakusei desu)

text: In Japanese, the standard polite word for 'I' is '{{c1::わたし}}' (Watashi).
explanation: In casual speech, males often use 'ぼく' (Boku) or 'おれ' (Ore). Females often use 'わたし' or 'あたし'.
usage: Polite: わたしはエンジニアです。 (I am an engineer.)
spanish: En japonés, la palabra estándar y cortés para 'yo' es 'わたし' (Watashi).

text: In Japanese, the word for 'you (singular)' is '{{c1::あなた}}' (Anata).
explanation: In Japanese culture, it is polite to address people by their name + '-san' rather than saying 'Anata' (which can sound overly direct or mean 'dear' when said by a spouse).
usage: Rule: Prefer name + さん (san) over あなた. Example: マルコさんは学生ですか？ (Is Marco a student?)
spanish: En japonés, 'tú' es 'あなた' (Anata), pero suele preferirse el nombre de la persona + 'san'.

text: In Japanese, the pronouns for 'he' and 'she' are '{{c1::かれ}}' (Kare) and '{{c2::かのじょ}}' (Kanojo).
explanation: 'Kanojo' also means 'girlfriend' and 'Kare' can mean 'boyfriend' depending on context.
usage: Examples: 彼は先生です。 (He is a teacher.) | 彼女は元気です。 (She is well.)
spanish: En japonés, los pronombres para 'él' y 'ella' son 'かれ' (Kare) y 'かのじょ' (Kanojo).

text: In Japanese, to make a pronoun plural, you add the suffix '{{c1::たち}}' (-tachi). For example, 'we' is '{{c2::わたしたち}}' (Watashitachi).
explanation: -tachi can be appended to personal pronouns or nouns representing people.
usage: Example: わたしたちは学生です。 (We are students.) | あなたたち (You all)
spanish: En japonés, para hacer plural un pronombre, se añade '-tachi'. 'Watashi' (yo) -> 'Watashitachi' (nosotros).

text: In Japanese, the polite copula meaning 'is / am / are' is '{{c1::です}}' (desu). Example: I am a student = 学生{{c2::です}}.
explanation: 'desu' is placed at the end of the sentence and serves as the predicate of state or identity.
usage: Usage: Place at end of noun/adjective sentences. 本です。 (It is a book.)
spanish: En japonés, la cópula cortés que equivale a 'ser/estar' es 'です' (desu). Se ubica al final.

text: In Japanese, the polite negative form of 'desu' (am not / is not / are not) is '{{c1::ではありません}}' (de wa arimasen) or casually '{{c2::じゃないです}}' (ja nai desu).
explanation: 'de wa arimasen' is formal written/spoken. 'ja nai desu' is common spoken.
usage: Formal: 学生ではありません。 | Casual: 学生じゃないです。 (I am not a student.)
spanish: En japonés, la forma negativa cortés de 'desu' es 'ではありません' (de wa arimasen) o 'じゃないです' (ja nai desu).

text: To make a question in Japanese, you add the question particle '{{c1::か}}' (ka) at the very end of the sentence.
explanation: Acts like a spoken question mark. You do not invert subject/verb or add auxiliary verbs.
usage: Example: 学生ですか？ (Are you a student?) | そうですか。 (Is that so?)
spanish: En japonés, las preguntas se forman añadiendo la partícula 'か' (ka) al final de la oración.

text: In Japanese, the topic marker particle is '{{c1::は}}' (pronounced 'wa'). Example: わたし{{c2::は}}学生です (As for me, I am a student).
explanation: Written with the character for 'ha' but pronounced as 'wa' when used as a particle.
usage: Rule: Subject/Topic + は + Predicate. Example: これは何ですか？ (What is this?)
spanish: En japonés, la partícula marcadora de tema es 'は' (se pronuncia 'wa').

text: In Japanese, the direct object marker particle is '{{c1::を}}' (pronounced 'o').
explanation: Written with 'wo' (を) but normally pronounced 'o'. It marks the target of transitive actions.
usage: Example: 水を飲みます。 (I drink water. [mizu = water, nomimasu = drink])
spanish: En japonés, la partícula marcadora de objeto directo es 'を' (se pronuncia 'o').

text: In Japanese, the possessive particle that acts like English apostrophe 's or 'of' is '{{c1::の}}' (no). Example: My book = わたし{{c2::の}}本.
explanation: Connects nouns. Noun 1 + の + Noun 2 -> Noun 2 belongs to Noun 1.
usage: Example: 日本のカメラ (Japanese camera) | 先生の本 (The teacher's book)
spanish: En japonés, la partícula posesiva o modificadora de sustantivos es 'の' (no). わたしの本 = Mi libro.

text: In Japanese, the words for 'this', 'that' (near listener), and 'that over there' (far from both) are '{{c1::これ}}' (Kore), '{{c2::それ}}' (Sore), and '{{c3::あれ}}' (Are).
explanation: These are demonstrative pronouns. The corresponding demonstrative adjectives (followed by a noun) are kono, sono, ano.
usage: Pronoun: これはペンです。 (This is a pen.) | Adjective: このペンはわたしのです。 (This pen is mine.)
spanish: En japonés: 'これ' (esto), 'それ' (eso) y 'あれ' (aquello).

text: In Japanese, what is the polite phrase to say 'My name is [Name]' using the copula? '[Name] {{c1::です}}' or '[Name] {{c2::と申します}}' (more formal).
explanation: Simply putting 'desu' after your name is the most common self-introduction. 'To moushimasu' translates to 'I am called...'.
usage: Example: ジーザスです。 (I am Jesus.)
spanish: Para decir 'Mi nombre es...' en japonés de forma sencilla: '[Nombre] です' o '[Nombre] と申します' (humilde/formal).

text: In Japanese, the particle meaning 'also' or 'too' is '{{c1::も}}' (mo), which replaces 'wa' or 'ga'. Example: Me too = わたし{{c2::も}}.
explanation: Identical in meaning to Spanish 'también'.
usage: Example: 彼も学生です。 (He is also a student.)
spanish: En japonés, la partícula que significa 'también' es 'も' (mo), la cual reemplaza a 'wa'.

text: In Japanese, the question word for 'What' is '{{c1::何}}' (pronounced 'nani' or 'nan').
explanation: 'nan' is used before 'desu' or counter words. 'nani' is used in other cases.
usage: Examples: 何ですか？ (What is it?) | 何を食べますか？ (What will you eat?)
spanish: En japonés, la palabra interrogativa para '¿Qué?' es '何' (nani / nan).

text: In Japanese, the question word for 'Who' is '{{c1::だれ}}' (Dare) or politely '{{c2::どなた}}' (Donata).
explanation: 'Donata' is used in business or formal situations.
usage: Example: あの人は誰ですか？ (Who is that person?)
spanish: En japonés, la palabra interrogativa para '¿Quién?' es 'だれ' (Dare) o formalmente 'どなた' (Donata).

text: In Japanese, what is the polite phrase meaning 'Nice to meet you' when exchanging names? {{c1::よろしくおねがいします}} (Yoroshiku onegaishimasu).
explanation: Literally: 'Please treat me well.' It's an indispensable greeting in self-introductions.
usage: Dialogue: - ジーザスです。どうぞよろしく。 (- I am Jesus. Nice to meet you.)
spanish: En japonés, la frase clave para cerrar una presentación y decir 'Un placer' es 'よろしくおねがいします'.
